wooden_shield.name: wooden shield
wooden_shield.lore: A rough disc of layered oak planks bound with iron strips at the rim. Though unglamorous, its bulk has turned aside countless blows from claws and rusted blades alike — the scratches and gouges in its face speak of many such meetings.

hide_shield.name: hide shield
hide_shield.lore: Layers of thick beast-hide, cured in tallow and stretched over a wicker frame. More resistant to weathering than wood and marginally tougher against animal claws, the hide shield is a common upgrade for explorers who haven't yet found metal.

iron_shield.name: iron shield
iron_shield.lore: Hammered iron beaten into a slightly domed profile for strength, with a riveted central boss for grip stability. Standard issue for militia and professional guards, its proven design has remained unchanged for three centuries.

bronze_shield.name: bronze shield
bronze_shield.lore: Cast bronze shaped into a wide convex disc, its curved face designed to deflect thrusting weapons at an angle. The alloy is softer than iron but denser, and its slight give on impact absorbs piercing force that would punch straight through a rigid plate.

steel_shield.name: steel shield
steel_shield.lore: Polished steel stretched over a shaped core, this kite shield is tempered to resist glancing blows. The slight flex in its face absorbs slash impacts before they can transfer fully to the wielder's arm.

mithril_shield.name: mithril shield
mithril_shield.lore: Beaten from a single piece of true mithril — a metal so light it seems to float yet harder than tempered steel — this shield feels almost weightless in the hand. Its crystalline inner structure scatters electrical discharges harmlessly across its surface in pale blue arcs.

crystal_shield.name: crystal shield
crystal_shield.lore: Ground from a single slab of spell-resonant crystal, this shield is almost perfectly transparent. Its lattice structure disperses incoming magical energy harmlessly, making it prized by those who face arcane opponents — and beautiful enough to be displayed in noble halls when not in use.

obsidian_shield.name: obsidian shield
obsidian_shield.lore: Ground from volcanic obsidian glass and reinforced with a web of iron banding, this shield is as much a magical ward as a physical barrier. The glass lattice partially refracts spell energies before they can cohere into damaging force, and its extreme hardness resists crushing impacts that dent ordinary metals.

dragonscale_shield.name: dragonscale shield
dragonscale_shield.lore: Built around overlapping scales from a fire dragon's underbelly — the toughest portion of an already near-indestructible hide. Fire washes over this shield like water off oil, and its iridescent surface shifts colors as though the dragon's spirit still lingers within.

adamantine_shield.name: adamantine shield
adamantine_shield.lore: A massive tower shield of pure adamantine, its surface an almost organic dark green-black. The metal is supernaturally dense — blunt weapons simply bounce off as though striking a mountain, though the weight demands exceptional constitution from its bearer.

aegis_of_athena.name: Aegis of Athena
aegis_of_athena.unidentified_name: a polished shield of supernatural brightness
aegis_of_athena.lore: The aegis was not originally a shield but a goatskin breastplate — or a thundercloud — worn by Zeus and Athena. Homer describes Athena shaking it during battle to produce fear in armies. The Gorgoneion — Medusa's severed head — was mounted on it after Perseus made his gift. Athena used a weapon whose power was entirely in what it made people see, rather than what it physically did.

svalinn_shield.name: Svalinn
svalinn_shield.unidentified_name: a shield that feels cool to the touch even in heat
svalinn_shield.lore: The Poetic Edda places Svalinn — the Cooler — before the sun to shield the earth from its full heat. Without it the mountains would burn and the seas boil. It stands there and no one knows how it arrived. The name means exactly what it does. The Norse naming conventions for cosmological entities were admirably functional.

pridwen.name: Pridwen
pridwen.unidentified_name: a white shield with a painted figure
pridwen.lore: Arthur's shield, named in the Welsh poem Pa gur and the Mabinogion. The image of the Virgin Mary was painted on its inner face, so Arthur fought facing her. This was the interior side — the side that faces the bearer. The image was not for showing to enemies. It was for Arthur to look at.

tower_shield_of_ajax.name: Tower Shield of Ajax
tower_shield_of_ajax.unidentified_name: a massive laminated shield of ox-hide and bronze
tower_shield_of_ajax.lore: Ajax's shield was described as a tower of seven ox-hides laminated together with bronze — the largest shield in the Iliad. He stood before the Greek ships and held back the Trojans by mass and will. Achilles was the brilliant warrior. Ajax was the one who could not be moved. When Odysseus was awarded Achilles' armour by rhetorical argument, Ajax's grief destroyed him. The shield outlasted its owner.

scutum_of_aeneas.name: Scutum of Aeneas
scutum_of_aeneas.unidentified_name: a curved rectangular shield of divine workmanship
scutum_of_aeneas.lore: Hephaestus forged a new shield for Aeneas with the entire future of Rome inscribed on its surface — battles not yet fought, emperors not yet born, the city not yet founded. Aeneas carried it without understanding what he looked at. He bore the fame and destiny of his descendants on his shoulders, ignorant of their names. Virgil considered this a beautiful image. It is also a description of every ancestor.

bronze_aegis.name: Bronze Aegis
bronze_aegis.lore: The ceremonial shield of a bronze giant, engraved with the eye of Hephaestus. Its curved surface deflects crushing blows better than any iron shield of its age.
